annotations.label: Decision theory

anchor.snippet: Decision theory or the theory of rational choice is a branch of probability

annotations.note: Decision theory as a discipline is not a formalized object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normative decision theory

anchor.snippet: Normative decision theory is concerned with identification of optimal decisions

annotations.note: Normative decision theory is a meta-level descriptor with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Descriptive decision theory

anchor.snippet: descriptive decision theory is concerned with describing observed behaviors

annotations.note: Descriptive decision theory is a behavioral/empirical concept outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Prescriptive decision theory

anchor.snippet: Prescriptive decision theory is concerned with predictions about behavior

annotations.note: Prescriptive decision theory is a methodological notion not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Expected value

anchor.snippet: the idea of expected value is that, when faced with a number of actions

annotations.note: The expectation of a random variable is captured by the Bochner integral against a probability measure, but Mathlib does not provide a dedicated decision-theoretic `expectedValue` definition.

annotations.label: St. Petersburg paradox / Dutch merchant

anchor.snippet: He gives an example in which a Dutch merchant is trying to decide whether to insure a cargo

annotations.note: The St. Petersburg paradox is a historical example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Expected utility maximization (von Neumann–Morgenstern)

anchor.snippet: proved that expected utility maximization followed from basic postulates about rational behavior

annotations.note: The von Neumann–Morgenstern utility representation theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Allais and Ellsberg paradoxes

anchor.snippet: showed that human behavior has systematic and sometimes important departures from expected-utility maximization

annotations.note: These behavioral economics paradoxes are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intertemporal choice

anchor.snippet: Intertemporal choice is concerned with the kind of choice where different actions lead to outcomes that are realized at different stages over time

annotations.note: Intertemporal choice is an economics concept absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heuristics

anchor.snippet: Heuristics are procedures for making a decision without working out the consequences of every option

annotations.note: Heuristics as a psychological concept are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gambler's fallacy

anchor.snippet: the gambler's fallacy — believing that an isolated random event is affected by previous isolated random events

annotations.note: The gambler's fallacy is a cognitive bias not formalized in Mathlib, though independence of events (`ProbabilityTheory.IndepFun`) is available.

annotations.label: Compromise effect

anchor.snippet: The compromise effect operates under a mindset that the most moderate option carries the most benefit

annotations.note: The compromise effect is a behavioral economics phenomenon absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete class theorems

anchor.snippet: the complete class theorems, which show that all admissible decision rules are equivalent to the Bayesian decision rule

annotations.note: Wald's complete class theorems on admissibility of Bayes decision rules are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ludic fallacy

anchor.snippet: called the ludic fallacy , is that there are inevitable imperfections in modeling the real world by particular models

annotations.note: The ludic fallacy is a philosophical critique not formalizable in Mathlib.
